events.title: Xia Dynasty Founded
events.detail: According to Chinese historical tradition, Yu the Great established the Xia Dynasty around 2070 BCE, marking the beginning of hereditary rule in China. The Xia is debated by archaeologists, but corresponds to the Erlitou culture (c. 1900–1500 BCE). Yu's flood-control engineering became foundational mythology for Chinese statecraft.

sources.name: Sima Qian, Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian)
Keightley, 'The Origins of Chinese Civilization' (UC Press)

events.title: Shang Dynasty: Oracle Bones & Bronze Age
events.detail: The Shang Dynasty (c. 1600–1046 BCE) is China's first archaeologically confirmed dynasty. Oracle bone inscriptions from Anyang provide the earliest Chinese writing. The Shang developed sophisticated bronze casting, a lunar calendar, and a hierarchical city-state system centered on Yin (modern Anyang).

events.title: Zhou Dynasty: Feudalism and the Mandate of Heaven
events.detail: King Wu of Zhou defeated the Shang at Muye, establishing the Zhou Dynasty (1046–256 BCE). Zhou kings justified rule through the Mandate of Heaven (Tianming), a concept central to Chinese political philosophy for millennia. The Zhou system of feudal enfeoffment (fengjian) created hundreds of vassal states that slowly fragmented into the Warring States.

events.title: Confucius: Founding of Confucian Philosophy
events.detail: Kong Qiu (Confucius) was born in the state of Lu (modern Shandong) in 551 BCE. His teachings on social harmony, filial piety, ritual propriety (li), and benevolent governance (ren) became the foundation of Chinese political and social thought. Confucianism shaped imperial governance, family structures, and East Asian civilization for 2,500 years.

events.title: Warring States Period & The Art of War
events.detail: The Warring States period (475–221 BCE) saw seven major states—Qin, Chu, Zhao, Wei, Han, Yan, and Qi—compete for dominance through constant warfare and diplomatic maneuvering. Sun Tzu's 'Art of War' (Bingfa) was composed during this era, becoming the world's most influential military treatise. The period also produced Legalism, Daoism, and Mohism.

sources.name: Lewis, 'Sanctioned Violence in Early China' (SUNY Press)

era: Imperial Unification: Qin & Han (221 BCE–220 CE)

events.title: Qin Shi Huang Unifies China
events.detail: Ying Zheng, King of Qin, completed conquest of all rival states in 221 BCE and proclaimed himself Qin Shi Huang (First Emperor). He standardized weights, measures, currency, and script; built the first Great Wall system; and created a centralized bureaucracy that became the template for all subsequent dynasties. His harsh Legalist rule lasted only 15 years before the dynasty collapsed.

events.title: Terracotta Army: Mausoleum of Qin Shi Huang
events.detail: Qin Shi Huang's mausoleum near Xi'an contains over 8,000 life-size terracotta warriors, 130 chariots, and 520 cavalry horses, rediscovered by farmers in 1974. The burial complex reflects the emperor's obsession with immortality and the scale of Qin imperial power. It remains one of archaeology's greatest discoveries.

events.title: Han Emperor Wudi Opens the Silk Road
events.detail: Emperor Wudi (r. 141–87 BCE) of the Han Dynasty dispatched diplomat Zhang Qian westward, establishing the Silk Road trade network linking China to Central Asia, Persia, Rome, and India. Silk, porcelain, tea, and spices flowed westward while Buddhism, glassware, and horses traveled east. The Han also pushed Chinese territory to its greatest extent, reaching the Tarim Basin.

sources.name: Ban Gu, Han Shu (Book of Han)
Hansen, 'The Silk Road: A New History' (Oxford University Press)

events.title: Cai Lun Invents Papermaking
events.detail: Court official Cai Lun refined the papermaking process using bark, hemp, rags, and fishnets, presenting his invention to Emperor He in 105 CE. Chinese papermaking spread along the Silk Road, reaching the Islamic world in 751 CE and Europe by the 13th century. This invention transformed global communication, administration, and intellectual culture.

sources.name: Fan Ye, Hou Han Shu (Book of Later Han)
Tsien, 'Paper and Printing,' Science and Civilisation in China Vol. 5 (Cambridge)

events.title: Three Kingdoms Period
events.detail: After the Han Dynasty collapsed, China fractured into the Three Kingdoms of Wei, Shu Han, and Wu (220–280 CE). The era's military rivalries—celebrated in the 14th-century novel 'Romance of the Three Kingdoms'—became foundational cultural mythology. The Battle of Red Cliffs (208 CE) where Cao Cao was defeated by the alliance of Liu Bei and Sun Quan remains China's most famous naval engagement.

sources.name: Chen Shou, Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms)

events.title: Tang Dynasty: China's Golden Age
events.detail: Li Yuan founded the Tang Dynasty in 618 CE, establishing Chang'an (modern Xi'an) as a cosmopolitan capital of perhaps 1 million residents. The Tang was China's most open and internationally connected imperial era—Buddhist pilgrims traveled to India, Persian merchants traded in Chang'an, and Chinese poetry (Tang shi) reached its peak under Du Fu and Li Bai. Tang legal codes and administrative systems influenced Japan, Korea, and Vietnam.

sources.name: Ouyang Xiu, Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tangshu)
Benn, 'Daily Life in Traditional China: The Tang Dynasty' (Hackett)

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion: Empire's Near-Collapse
events.detail: General An Lushan launched a devastating rebellion in 755 CE that nearly destroyed the Tang Dynasty. The resulting warfare, famine, and displacement killed an estimated 13–36 million people (one-sixth of world population). The Tang never fully recovered, devolving into regional warlordism. The rebellion is considered one of history's deadliest conflicts.

sources.name: White, 'The World of the Middle Ages' (Princeton)

events.title: Song Dynasty: Gunpowder, Printing, and the Compass
events.detail: The Song Dynasty (960–1279) produced China's greatest technological revolution: movable-type printing (Bi Sheng, c. 1040), gunpowder weapons in warfare, the magnetic compass for navigation, and paper money. Song China had the world's largest economy (~25–30% of global GDP). Its commercialized, urbanized society and vibrant tea and silk trade made it the most prosperous civilization of the medieval era.

sources.name: Needham, 'Science and Civilisation in China' (Cambridge University Press)
Ebrey, 'The Cambridge Illustrated History of China' (Cambridge)

events.title: Mongol Conquest: Kublai Khan Founds Yuan Dynasty
events.detail: Kublai Khan completed the Mongol conquest of the Southern Song in 1279 after decades of warfare and founded the Yuan Dynasty in 1271, making China the core of the Mongol Empire. The conquest caused immense destruction and demographic collapse in northern China. However, Kublai's court at Khanbaliq (Beijing) attracted Marco Polo and fostered trans-Eurasian trade. The Yuan also attempted invasions of Japan (1274, 1281) and Vietnam—all failed.

sources.name: Marco Polo, 'The Travels of Marco Polo'

events.title: Zheng He's Treasure Fleet Voyages
events.detail: Admiral Zheng He led seven massive naval expeditions (1405–1433) commanding fleets of up to 300 ships and 28,000 crew, reaching Southeast Asia, India, Arabia, and the East African coast. These voyages demonstrated Chinese maritime supremacy decades before European exploration. The Yongle Emperor's death and Confucian officials' hostility ended the voyages, leading China to turn inward—a pivotal missed opportunity.

sources.name: Dreyer, 'Zheng He: China and the Oceans in the Early Ming' (Pearson)

events.title: Ming Dynasty Rebuilds the Great Wall
events.detail: The Ming Dynasty (1368–1644), founded by Zhu Yuanzhang after expelling the Mongols, undertook the most extensive construction of the Great Wall in history. Most of the iconic wall visible today was built by the Ming, stretching over 8,850 km and employing millions of conscripts. The wall reflected Ming anxiety about northern nomadic threats following the Mongol occupation.

sources.name: Waldron, 'The Great Wall of China' (Cambridge University Press)

events.title: Manchu Qing Dynasty Conquers China
events.detail: The Manchu Qing Dynasty seized Beijing in 1644 after Ming loyalist general Wu Sangui opened the Shanhai Pass to defeat Li Zicheng's rebel forces. The Qing imposed distinctive cultural markers—the queue hairstyle under pain of death—while adopting Chinese bureaucratic systems. Under Kangxi (r. 1661–1722) and Qianlong (r. 1735–1796), the Qing expanded to its greatest territorial extent, incorporating Tibet, Xinjiang, and Taiwan.

sources.name: Spence, 'The Search for Modern China' (W.W. Norton)
Struve (ed.), 'The Qing Formation in World-Historical Time' (Harvard)

events.title: First Opium War: China's Century of Humiliation Begins
events.detail: Britain's Royal Navy crushed Chinese coastal defenses (1839–1842) after China attempted to stop the British East India Company's profitable opium trade. The Treaty of Nanjing (1842) ceded Hong Kong to Britain, opened five treaty ports, and imposed £21 million in reparations. This launched China's 'Century of Humiliation' (百年屈辱)—a period of unequal treaties, territorial losses, and foreign-imposed concessions that remains central to Chinese national identity.

sources.name: Fairbank, 'China: A New History' (Harvard University Press)

events.title: Taiping Rebellion: World's Deadliest Civil War
events.detail: Hong Xiuquan, claiming to be Jesus's brother, led the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom against the Qing (1850–1864). The rebellion engulfed southern China, capturing Nanjing as its capital (1853). An estimated 20–30 million people died from combat, famine, and disease, making it the deadliest civil conflict in history before World War II. The Qing survived only with help from the Western-commanded Ever Victorious Army and regional militias.

events.title: Boxer Rebellion & Eight-Nation Alliance
events.detail: The Boxer Uprising (1899–1901) saw anti-foreign Chinese militia besiege the Beijing Legation Quarter. Empress Dowager Cixi declared war on foreign powers, leading to a joint intervention by Britain, France, Germany, Russia, Japan, the US, Austria-Hungary, and Italy. The resulting Boxer Protocol (1901) imposed 450 million taels (~$333 million) in reparations, deepening China's humiliation and financial subjugation.

sources.name: Cohen, 'History in Three Keys: The Boxers as Event, Experience, and Myth' (Columbia)

events.title: Xinhai Revolution: End of Imperial China
events.detail: The Wuchang Uprising (October 10, 1911) triggered a nationwide revolution that toppled the Qing Dynasty. Sun Yat-sen became provisional president of the Republic of China on January 1, 1912. The last emperor Puyi abdicated on February 12, ending 2,132 years of imperial rule. The revolution reflected decades of reformist frustration with Qing inability to modernize against Western and Japanese pressure.

sources.name: Schiffrin, 'Sun Yat-sen and the Origins of the Chinese Revolution' (UC Press)

events.title: May Fourth Movement: Birth of Chinese Nationalism
events.detail: On May 4, 1919, thousands of Beijing students protested the Versailles Treaty's transfer of German concessions in Shandong to Japan rather than China. The movement galvanized Chinese nationalism and intellectualism, sparking debates over democracy, science, Marxism, and cultural revolution. Future CCP founders including Mao Zedong were radicalized by this moment. May Fourth remains a touchstone for both CCP legitimacy claims and liberal reform movements.

sources.name: Chow, 'The May Fourth Movement' (Harvard University Press)

events.title: Chinese Communist Party Founded in Shanghai
events.detail: Thirteen delegates including Mao Zedong met secretly in Shanghai in July 1921 to found the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), with Soviet Comintern support. The party began with fewer than 60 members. By 1949 it would command a 4-million-strong army and rule the world's most populous country. The founding site in the French Concession is now a major patriotic education museum in modern Shanghai.

sources.name: Pantsov & Levine, 'Mao: The Real Story' (Simon & Schuster)

events.title: The Long March: CCP's Founding Myth
events.detail: Encircled by Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist forces, 86,000 Communist troops under Mao Zedong embarked on the Long March (October 1934–October 1935), covering ~9,000 km across 11 provinces through treacherous mountains. Only ~8,000 survived. The march cemented Mao's leadership and became the CCP's founding myth—the equivalent of the American Revolution for Chinese Communist legitimacy. Survivors became the 'immortal generation' of PRC leadership.

sources.name: CCP History Research Office, 'A Brief History of the CCP'

events.title: Second Sino-Japanese War & Nanjing Massacre
events.detail: Japan's full-scale invasion of China began July 7, 1937 (Marco Polo Bridge Incident). The fall of Nanjing in December 1937 was followed by the Nanjing Massacre: Japanese troops killed an estimated 200,000–300,000 civilians and POWs over six weeks. Total Chinese casualties in the war (1937–1945) reached 15–20 million. The conflict remains central to Sino-Japanese relations and Chinese national memory.

sources.name: Memorial Hall for Victims of the Nanjing Massacre by Japanese Invaders
International Military Tribunal for the Far East

events.title: PRC Founded: Mao Proclaims New China
events.detail: On October 1, 1949, Mao Zedong stood at Tiananmen Gate and proclaimed the People's Republic of China, declaring 'The Chinese people have stood up.' The CCP's victory in the civil war drove Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist government to Taiwan. The PRC immediately aligned with the Soviet Union (Sino-Soviet Treaty, 1950) and began land reform, nationalization of industry, and mass political campaigns against 'class enemies.'

sources.name: Spence, 'The Search for Modern China' (W.W. Norton)

events.title: China Enters Korean War: 'Resist America, Aid Korea'
events.detail: As UN forces approached the Yalu River in October 1950, Mao ordered 300,000 People's Volunteer Army troops into Korea, pushing allied forces back to the 38th parallel. Chinese losses are officially acknowledged at 197,653 killed; Western estimates range up to 400,000. The war shaped US-China hostility for decades and reinforced PRC domestic legitimacy. A ceasefire was signed July 27, 1953.

events.title: Great Leap Forward: Catastrophic Famine
events.detail: Mao's Great Leap Forward (1958–1962) aimed to rapidly industrialize China through mass mobilization—backyard steel furnaces, collective farms, and impossibly high grain quotas. Unrealistic targets, falsified production reports, and political terror against those who reported failures caused the worst famine in human history. An estimated 15–55 million people died. The CCP officially acknowledges 'serious losses' but has never released comprehensive data.

events.title: Cultural Revolution: A Decade of Chaos
events.detail: Mao launched the Cultural Revolution in May 1966 to purge 'capitalist roaders' and reassert his authority after the Great Leap's failures. Red Guards attacked traditional culture, intellectuals, party officials, and religious institutions. Schools and universities closed for years. An estimated 500,000 to 2 million died in political violence; tens of millions were persecuted. The Cultural Revolution ended with Mao's death in 1976 and was later officially condemned by the CCP.

sources.name: MacFarquhar & Schoenhals, 'Mao's Last Revolution' (Harvard University Press)

events.title: Nixon Opens China: 'The Week That Changed the World'
events.detail: US President Richard Nixon's historic visit to China (February 21–28, 1972) broke 22 years of US-PRC mutual non-recognition. The Shanghai Communiqué acknowledged the 'one China' position without explicitly endorsing it, laying the diplomatic groundwork for China's emergence from isolation. Nixon's visit was facilitated by National Security Adviser Henry Kissinger and was driven by strategic desire to exploit the Sino-Soviet split.

events.title: Death of Mao Zedong: End of an Era
events.detail: Mao Zedong died on September 9, 1976, aged 82, after years of declining health from ALS. His death ended 27 years of personal rule that had reshaped China through land reform, industrialization, famine, and political terror. Within weeks, the Gang of Four (including Mao's wife Jiang Qing) were arrested. The succession struggle between Hua Guofeng and Deng Xiaoping set the stage for China's transformation into a market economy.

events.title: Deng Xiaoping's Reform and Opening Up
events.detail: At the Third Plenary Session of the 11th Central Committee (December 1978), Deng Xiaoping outmaneuvered Hua Guofeng and launched 'Reform and Opening Up' (改革开放). Deng decollectivized agriculture, allowed private enterprise, and created Special Economic Zones—sparking the most rapid economic growth in history. China's GDP grew from $149 billion in 1978 to $1.2 trillion in 2000. Deng famously declared 'To get rich is glorious.'

sources.name: CCP Central Committee Third Plenary Communiqué (1978)
Vogel, 'Deng Xiaoping and the Transformation of China' (Harvard University Press)

events.title: Tiananmen Square Massacre
events.detail: Student-led pro-democracy protests occupied Tiananmen Square from April 15 to June 4, 1989, inspiring similar demonstrations across 400 Chinese cities. On June 3–4, Deng Xiaoping ordered the military to clear the square. Tanks rolled down Chang'an Avenue; soldiers fired into crowds. Death toll estimates range from 241 (Chinese government) to 2,700 (Chinese Red Cross estimate, later retracted) to 10,000 (declassified UK diplomatic cable). The massacre remains the most censored event in China.

sources.name: Amnesty International, 'People's Republic of China: Preliminary Findings' (1989)
Declassified FCO Cable, Sir Alan Donald (1989)

events.title: Hong Kong Handover: 'One Country, Two Systems'
events.detail: At midnight on July 1, 1997, Britain transferred sovereignty of Hong Kong to the PRC after 156 years of colonial rule. Deng Xiaoping's 'One Country, Two Systems' formula promised Hong Kong a high degree of autonomy, independent judiciary, and civil liberties for 50 years. The handover was preceded by mass emigration of wealthy Hong Kongers. The subsequent erosion of Hong Kong's autonomy culminated in the 2019 protests and 2020 National Security Law.

events.title: China Joins WTO: Integration into Global Economy
events.detail: China's accession to the World Trade Organization on December 11, 2001 after 15 years of negotiations transformed global trade. WTO membership accelerated China's export manufacturing boom: exports grew from $266 billion (2001) to $2.4 trillion (2013). China became the world's largest trading nation by 2013. Critics argue China's state subsidies, currency management, and market access restrictions violate WTO principles.

events.title: Beijing Olympics: China's Coming-Out Party
events.detail: China hosted the 2008 Summer Olympics (August 8–24) in Beijing, presenting its economic rise to the world with a spectacular $43 billion investment in infrastructure and the iconic 'Bird's Nest' stadium. China won 51 gold medals—the most of any nation. The games were preceded by protests over Tibet suppression and boycott threats over Darfur, but proceeded without incident. The global financial crisis erupted two weeks after the closing ceremony.

sources.name: Beijing Organizing Committee for the Olympic Games (BOCOG)

events.title: Sichuan Earthquake: 69,000 Dead, 'Tofu Construction' Scandal
events.detail: A magnitude 7.9 earthquake struck Sichuan province on May 12, 2008, killing 69,197 people and injuring 374,000. The disproportionate collapse of schools—versus adjacent government buildings—revealed systemic corruption in 'tofu construction' (豆腐渣工程). Parents who organized to demand accountability were imprisoned. The disaster nonetheless prompted an unprecedented national outpouring of grief and volunteerism.

events.title: Xi Jinping Rises to Power
events.detail: Xi Jinping became General Secretary of the CCP on November 15, 2012, and President of the PRC in March 2013. Unlike his predecessors, Xi moved rapidly to consolidate personal power, launching a sweeping anti-corruption campaign, abolishing presidential term limits (2018), and enshrining 'Xi Jinping Thought' in the constitution. Xi is considered China's most powerful leader since Mao, personally overseeing military, economic, and foreign policy.

sources.name: Economy, 'The Third Revolution: Xi Jinping and the New Chinese State' (Oxford)

events.title: Belt and Road Initiative Launched
events.detail: Xi Jinping announced the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) in 2013—a massive infrastructure investment across Asia, Africa, Europe, and Latin America. By 2024, over 150 countries had signed agreements. Total estimated Chinese investment: $1 trillion+. Critics describe BRI as 'debt-trap diplomacy'—several countries (Sri Lanka, Pakistan, Zambia) have faced debt distress from Chinese loans, while Beijing argues BRI drives development.

sources.name: Chinese Ministry of Foreign Affairs, BRI Office

events.title: Xinjiang: Mass Detention of Uyghurs
events.detail: From 2017, China rapidly expanded a network of internment facilities in Xinjiang, detaining an estimated 1 million+ Uyghurs, Kazakhs, and Turkic Muslims without trial. Leaked government documents (China Cables, Xinjiang Papers) reveal systematic cultural erasure, forced labor, and political indoctrination. Beijing calls facilities 'vocational education centers' for counter-terrorism. The US, UK, EU, and Canada have passed legislation labeling China's actions genocide or crimes against humanity.

sources.name: Australian Strategic Policy Institute (ASPI), 'The Xinjiang Data Project'
Chinese State Council, 'Vocational Education and Training in Xinjiang' white paper

events.title: Hong Kong Protests: Millions Take to Streets
events.detail: A proposed extradition bill to mainland China triggered the largest protest movement in Hong Kong's history, with over 2 million marching on June 16, 2019. Protests continued for months, with police firing 16,000+ rounds of tear gas and 6,000+ arrests. Beijing's response bypassed the Hong Kong government, imposing a National Security Law in June 2020 that criminalized secession, subversion, and collusion with foreign forces—effectively ending Hong Kong's political opposition.

sources.name: Amnesty International, 'Hong Kong: Year of Unrest' (2020)

events.title: COVID-19: Pandemic Emerges in Wuhan
events.detail: A novel coronavirus (SARS-CoV-2) emerged in Wuhan, Hubei province in late 2019. Chinese authorities delayed public warnings and silenced doctors including Li Wenliang, who was forced to sign a document admitting 'disturbing public order.' WHO declared a pandemic on March 11, 2020. China's strict zero-COVID policy (2020–2022) and the subsequent abrupt abandonment caused millions of deaths. COVID origins remain disputed: WHO investigation stalled; US intelligence agencies diverge on lab-leak vs. zoonotic spillover.

sources.name: WHO, 'COVID-19: Making Sense of It All' (2020)
CGTN, 'China's COVID-19 Prevention and Control Measures'

events.title: Xi Jinping's Unprecedented Third Term
events.detail: At the 20th CCP National Congress (October 2022), Xi Jinping secured a third five-year term as General Secretary, breaking with the post-Mao norm of two terms. Xi removed rivals from the Politburo Standing Committee and replaced them with loyalists. Former president Hu Jintao was visibly removed from the Congress floor in a widely filmed moment. Xi's consolidation of power positions him as paramount leader potentially for life.

sources.name: Xinhua News Agency, 20th CCP National Congress
MERICS, 'China's Leadership After the 20th Party Congress' (2022)

events.title: Taiwan Strait Crisis Intensifies
events.detail: PLA military exercises around Taiwan escalated dramatically in 2024. Joint Sword-2024A (May) and Joint Sword-2024B (October) simulated blockade and strike operations following Taiwan's new president William Lai Ching-te's inauguration. Monthly PLA aircraft incursions into Taiwan's ADIZ doubled from ~141/month (2023) to ~300/month (2024). A single October 2024 operation involved 153 aircraft. The US responded with $8 billion in Taiwan arms sales.

sources.name: Taiwan Ministry of National Defense daily reports (2024)
Al Jazeera, 'Taiwan Reports Surrounded by 153 Chinese Military Aircraft,' October 2024
PLA Eastern Theater Command official statements

events.title: Real Estate Crisis: Evergrande Collapses
events.detail: China's property sector, which once accounted for ~25% of GDP, entered a severe crisis. Evergrande—with over $300 billion in debt—was ordered liquidated by a Hong Kong court in January 2024. Country Garden, another giant developer, also defaulted. Housing prices fell in all major cities; developer defaults left millions of pre-sold homes unbuilt ('rotten tail buildings'). Beijing's stimulus measures have partially stabilized prices but structural overcapacity remains unresolved.

sources.name: IMF World Economic Outlook, China Chapter (2024)
